provenance.source_corpus: Vegetation Control Treatments to Favor Naturally Regenerated Betula alleghaniensis Saplings Following Seed‐Tree Cut: Sapling Monitoring Two Years after Treatment

source_spans.text: Four contrasting vegetation control treatments were tested to determine their effect on the growth and vigor of eight-year-old B. alleghaniensis saplings regenerating after ﬁnal cutting of a shelterwood seed cut.
Two years after treatment application, diameter growth signiﬁcantly improved in response to vegetation control treatments, whereas sapling height growth did not.
Two years after treatment application, diameter growth signiﬁcantly improved in response to vegetation control treatments, whereas sapling height growth did not.
Mean etiolation index decreased signiﬁcantly with increasing vegetation control, though TC and PC treatments were not signiﬁcantly different (Table 2; Fig. 1D).
However, increasing the severity of vegetation removal also made saplings more conspicuous to herbivores, which increased browsing, especially in the TC and PC treatments.
Survival was very high in all vegetation control treatments, despite a signiﬁcantly lower sapling survival in the TS compared to the TC treatment (Table 2; Fig. 1A).

edges.original_sentence: Diameter growth was negatively correlated to per-plot browsing proportion in the most severe vegetation control treatments TC and PC, but the relationship was not signiﬁcant for PC (Table 3).
